AI-Powered ICP Scoring
for Smarter Sales Targeting

We built a machine learning pipeline that identifies which companies are most likely to align with LEAD.bot's vision — before any sales contact is made. The model scores any company purely from firmographic signals: industry, company size, seniority, and function. No CRM history required.

The Core Problem This Solves
Sales teams waste time on leads that will never align with the product vision. This model tells you — before the first call — how closely a company matches the profile of leads that have already validated the vision.
Key finding: Company size is the single strongest predictor of ICP fit. Leads in the 200–1,499 employee range are confirmed as ICP at 3.5× the baseline rate. At this scale, organizations have developed knowledge silos but haven't yet built dedicated knowledge infrastructure — the exact condition LEAD.bot is designed for.
Counter-intuitive finding: The largest inbound segment converts at below the baseline rate. The model correctly down-scores this segment. High inbound volume ≠ high fit. Redirecting effort toward knowledge-intensive sectors significantly improves expected return.
Model design principle: Leads that haven't converted yet are treated as unlabeled — not negative. A company that looks like a confirmed ICP but hasn't purchased is a targeting opportunity, not a failure signal. The model scores similarity, not just past conversion.
ICP Score Distribution — All Leads
ICP-confirmed leads (green) cluster at higher scores. Unlabeled leads (gray) spread across the range — some are strong candidates waiting to be reached.

🤖

📊
Score
0–100 ICP score for every lead + new prospects
Positive-Unlabeled (PU) Learning
The most important modeling decision in this project — and the most common mistake to avoid.
❌ Naive approach (wrong)

Label all non-converting leads as converted = 0. Train a binary classifier. The model learns to penalize companies that look like good ICP leads just because they haven't converted yet — a self-defeating loop.

✓ PU Learning (correct)

Label vision-validated leads as positive = 1. Treat all others as unlabeled — not negative. Score every lead by similarity to the confirmed ICP cluster. Proxy negatives are drawn only from leads that are clearly dissimilar, not from the unlabeled pool.

Why this matters: A lead that hasn't responded to outreach yet might be a perfect ICP — wrong timing, wrong contact, or the outreach simply hasn't reached them yet. Penalizing them as "negative" teaches the model to down-score your best future customers.
ICP Label Definition
Two types of leads contribute to the positive (confirmed ICP) pool.
Signal TypeRationale
Vision-validated leadsLeads that committed to the product — direct evidence of ICP fit and problem recognition
Former users who moved onLeads that engaged but later disengaged — still strong ICP signal. Disengagement reflects product maturity, timing, or internal organizational factors, not ICP mismatch. A lead that recognized the problem and committed is confirmed ICP regardless of current status.
On disengagement: When a confirmed ICP lead disengages, three patterns account for the majority of cases: (1) the internal champion changed roles, (2) budget ownership shifted, or (3) the organization wasn't structurally ready to adopt. None of these invalidate the ICP signal. The company still has the problem — the timing or internal conditions changed.
Feature Engineering
The ICP model uses 9 problem-driven firmographic features — all derivable from publicly available data. Platform and geographic region were intentionally excluded: they reflect current sales reach, not problem fit. The model should generalize to any market, not just where sales has already been active.
FeatureTypeConstructionSignal
Size Band M (200–1,499 emp)BinaryHeadcount midpoint falls in silo-formation rangeVery Strong
Knowledge IndustryBinaryRegulated Services, Public Institutions, Professional Services, Academic & Research, Life Sciences, IndustrialStrong
Buyer PersonaBinaryDirector+ seniority AND (People/HR or IT/Ops function in job title)Strong
People / HR FunctionBinaryHR, People, Culture, Talent, L&D keywords — word-boundary regexGood
IT / Ops FunctionBinaryIT, Operations, Systems, Infrastructure keywords — word-boundary regexGood
Seniority ScoreNumeric 0–5Executive=5, VP=4, Director=3, Manager=2, IC=1 — regex with word boundaries to avoid substring false matchesGood
Headcount (raw)NumericMidpoint of reported size rangeModerate
Technology SectorBinaryTech / SaaS industry — acts as a negative signal in this datasetWeak (negative)
RecencyNumericDays since last recorded contact — more recent = higher engagement signalContextual
Feature Importance — PU Random Forest
Which features drive ICP score most. Derived from the PU-trained Random Forest using mean decrease in impurity.
SHAP Feature Importance — Pipeline Model
SHapley Additive exPlanations. Each dot = one lead. Red = high feature value pushing score up, Blue = pushing down.
Two ICP Sub-Segments (K-Means Clustering)
K-Means clustering on confirmed ICP leads revealed two distinct buyer archetypes with different pitch angles.
SegmentShareRegionIndustryBuyerSizePitch Angle
Core Segment ~72%Region ARegulated Services, Professional Services Director200–3,000 Knowledge retention, cross-team pairing, onboarding speed
Secondary Segment ~28%Region BAcademic & Research, Public Institutions Manager-level200–500 Staff development, policy knowledge, L&D programs
Models Trained & Why
Six classifiers were trained and compared. SMOTE was applied to handle class imbalance during training. A separate evaluation was run on the real class distribution — no synthetic data — to produce honest accuracy metrics.

XGBoost

Best Overall

Gradient boosting on decision trees. Handles class imbalance well via scale_pos_weight. Best generalization on the real test set.

0.89
ROC-AUC
0.77
PR-AUC
0.98
CV Score

LightGBM

Best Overall

Leaf-wise gradient boosting. Faster training; matched XGBoost performance. Preferred when scoring speed matters at scale.

0.89
ROC-AUC
0.77
PR-AUC

Random Forest

Strong

Ensemble of decision trees. Robust to outliers. Used as the base model for SHAP explainability and PU learning.

0.889
ROC-AUC
0.77
PR-AUC

Logistic Regression

Baseline

Linear model used as interpretable baseline. Lower AUC confirms the non-linear models are capturing real structure, not noise.

0.64
ROC-AUC

Gradient Boosting

Strong

Scikit-learn implementation. Consistent performer with no additional dependencies. Good for production environments.

~0.87
ROC-AUC

K-Means Clustering

Segmentation

Used for ICP sub-segment discovery, not scoring. Identified two distinct buyer archetypes with different pitch angles.

2
Segments

Full evaluation suite. Confusion matrix: 111 true negatives, 3 true positives, 1 false negative, 0 false positives on the held-out test set.
Cohort Lift Analysis
All leads were split into cohorts by industry sector, company size, seniority, and region. Each cohort's ICP confirmation rate is compared against the overall baseline. Lift = how many times more likely that cohort is to be ICP-confirmed vs. the average lead.
4.9×
Top industry lift
Public Institutions
4.6×
Top region lift
Region B
3.5×
Top size lift
Size Band M (200–1,499)
3.0×
2nd industry lift
Regulated Services
2.7×
Top seniority lift
Director level
ICP Confirmation Rate by Segment vs Baseline
Green = strong lift (≥2.5×) · Purple = good (1.5–2.5×) · Yellow = neutral · Red = below baseline
Cross-Segment Heatmap — Seniority × Industry & Size × Industry
Cells with fewer than 3 leads shown as —. Color scale: green = high ICP rate, red = low.
Key Findings
Knowledge-intensive sectors outperform
Public Institutions, Regulated Services, Academic & Research, and Professional Services all convert at 3–5× the baseline. These sectors share a common pressure: distributed institutional knowledge, compliance requirements, and high staff turnover — exactly the conditions that make structured knowledge sharing critical.
Mid-market is the size sweet spot
Size Band M (200–1,499 employees) confirms at 3.5× baseline. Large enough to have developed silos across teams, small enough for a Director-level decision without 12-month procurement cycles.
Director is the buyer
Director-level contacts confirm at 2.7× baseline. They have both budget authority and direct exposure to the knowledge loss problem. C-Suite confirms at 2.0× — strong but often delegates the problem downward.
High inbound ≠ high fit
The highest-volume inbound segment confirms at below baseline. This segment feels less pain because they have existing internal tooling and engineering capacity. The model correctly assigns them lower scores — redirecting effort toward knowledge-intensive sectors improves expected return significantly.
The combined ICP profile — Director+ at a 200–1,499 employee organization in a knowledge-intensive sector (Regulated Services, Public Institutions, Academic & Research, or Professional Services), in Region A or B. Estimated ICP confirmation rate for this combined profile: 6–8× the overall baseline.
ICP Scoring — How It Works
The ICP score (0–100) is an ensemble of three signals blended together. All inputs are publicly available — no CRM history, no prior relationship required.
40%
Cosine Similarity

Distance from the confirmed ICP centroid in normalized feature space

40%
PU Random Forest

Probability estimate from model trained on confirmed positives + proxy negatives

20%
Isolation Forest

Anomaly detection trained exclusively on confirmed ICP leads — measures how "typical" a lead looks

Score Tier Guide
ScoreTierRecommended Action
75–100Strong ICPImmediate personalized outreach. Reference their specific role and the known pain point for their sector and size.
55–74Likely ICPNurture sequence. Follow up within one week of any engagement signal.
35–54Weak ICPAutomated content only. Treat as warm if they initiate contact.
0–34Low SignalNo active sales time. Monitor for firmographic changes that could shift the score.

Inputs required to score any company: Industry sector · Approximate headcount · Contact job title. All three are available from a company website or professional network profile. No prior relationship, no CRM access, no API integration required at scoring time.
# Score any company from the command line python3 -c " from icp_v3 import score_new_company score_new_company( job_title='Director of People Operations', industry='Financial Services', company_size=450, ) "
